text:
Community News Editor / Librarian

NORTH ADAMS — When Fred T. Thompson laces up his running shoes Saturday morning, he will be facing a truly Olympian task.

Thompson, 43, is one of more than 4,500 runners who will carry the traditional Olympic flame across one small stretch of this country en route to the Summer Olympic Games in Los Angeles.

The Sprague Electric Co. executive will bear the torch for six or seven minutes Saturday as he winds through a portion of the United States Military Academy at West Point, N.Y.

The flame, which originated in Olympia, Greece, arrived Tuesday at the United Nations building in New York. It will eventually pass through 33 states on its 82-day journey to Los Angeles. Its arrival at the Summer Olympics site is scheduled for 7 p.m. on July 28, according to an Olympic torch relay spokesman.

Thompson is being sponsored by Sprague, which contributed $3,000 for his one kilometer run. He is one of 4,000 Youth Legacy Kilometer (YLK) runners who were each sponsored to carry the flame for sixth-tenths of a mile.

The money raised through YLK donations will go toward "promoting future young Olympians," according to Steve Barr, who is part of an advance team coordinating the cross-country torch relay.

Runners or their sponsors could earmark YLK contributions for the Boys' Club or Girls' Club of America, or for the YMCA. Sprague's contribution will go to the Girls' Club, Thompson said. Mary Jane Sprague, wife of company president John L. Sprague, was president of the Girls' Club of America for four years.

Segments of the torch route not covered by YLK participants will be run by over 500 employees of American Telephone & Telegraph Co., which is sponsoring the entire relay, Barr said.

Thompson was chosen to represent Sprague after he placed first among the company's New England employees in a race held last October, he said.

The North Adams native, a well-rounded athlete, said he does not plan on dashing over his segment of the relay. He said he anticipates running 1 kilometer in about six minutes.

"The slower I go, the longer I get to carry the flame," he remarked.

This Story in History is selected from the archives by Jeannie Maschino, The Berkshire Eagle.

Community News Editor / Librarian

Eagle Archives, Dec. 6, 1938: Older Pittsfield residents worry that construction of the new Allen Street police station may uncover graves from the former town cemetery, which once occupied part of the site.

In the late 1950s and through the mid-1960s there was a flurry of corny songs, many with nonsense word refrains.

Eagle Archives, Dec. 5, 1952: The Pittsfield Fire Department banned a highly flammable liquid hair spray after reports from New York showed it could ignite like a blowtorch.

Eagle Archives, Dec. 4, 1963: John E. Callaghan, a WNAC radio and TV announcer from Pittsfield, spent three days in Ireland to gather reactions to President Kennedy’s assassination.

Eagle Archives, Dec. 3, 1973: The first gasless Sunday brought quiet roads and crowded TVs in the Berkshires, as residents stayed home after scrambling to fill up their gas tanks the day before.

Eagle Archives, Dec. 2, 1952: A mail order business originated when guests at Music Inn inquired where they might purchase some of the unusual food items served at the inn.

author: Julie Sell
